text: The video shows a fluorescent colorectal anastomosis in a patient undergoing high anterior resection for sigmoid cancer. The colon appears unhealthy, indicating that the anastomosis requires revision.

The video shows a laparoscopic resection being performed on a 62-year-old male patient with sigmoid cancer. The surgeon uses a medial to lateral approach, completing a distal transection across the upper rectum.

The surgeon prepares the left mesocolon intercorporeally following the high ligation of the inferior mesenteric artery. The marginal artery perfusion appears adequate, which will be confirmed using indocyanine green (ICG) angiography.

The surgeon uses the Novadax endoscopic system, Pinpoint, to perform perfusion angiography. The near-infrared view shows ICG inflow proximally to the designated point for proximal transection, revealing a good arterial inflow pattern consistent with brisk perfusion in the marginal artery.

The surgeon uses a stapler to perform the proximal transection, ensuring the cut is made well within the area of good perfusion as indicated by the fluorescence inflow, avoiding the junction between well-perfused and malperfused tissue.

The surgeon performs relaparoscopy after specimen removal, preparing the proximal colon with a purse string and anvil. The anvil is visible in the proximal descending colon, being aligned with the head of the circular stapler, which is inserted transanally into the rectum.

The surgeon switches to the near infrared view to perform ICG angiography of the anastomosis in situ. The colon tissue surrounding the anvil shows delayed fluorescence compared to adjacent tissues, gradually brightening over thirty seconds. The black and white view in the middle left of the screen highlights this fluorescence pattern.
The surgeon fires the stapler to complete the transection. The team then inspects the surgical site to identify any issues with the anastomosis.

The surgeon examines the bowel, noting patchy fluorescence in the distal segment compared to the brighter proximal colon. The mesocolon is inspected, revealing an area of encroachment potentially caused during splenic flexure mobilization or specimen extraction, affecting venous outflow while preserving marginal artery inflow.

The surgeon examines the bowel under white light, noting some arterial perfusion but identifying issues with tension or traction that may affect the anastomosis. Despite fluorescence suggesting adequate perfusion, the white light view reveals compromised tissue health in the colon segment.

The near-infrared view shows that over twenty minutes following ICG injection, the affected segment of the colon retains fluorescence, indicating venous congestion. The ICG, which should have cleared, remains trapped, highlighting venous ischemia in this area.
The surgeon revises the anastomosis on the affected segment of the colon, which appears compromised due to venous congestion and inadequate perfusion.

The surgeon performs a proximal transection across the descending colon, creating a new proximal colonic conduit. Subsequently, a further transection is made in the distal sigmoid colon, providing a clear view and easy access to the colorectal anastomosis site.

The surgeon performs further dissection of the mesorectum to ensure the distal transection is below the previous staple line. The rectal stump is freshened to prepare for the second anastomosis, ensuring implantation into healthy rectal tissue.
The surgeon begins redoing the anastomosis by inserting the circular stapler head, with the spike visibly emerging through the rectal stump.

The surgeon identifies the proximal colonic segment as the very proximal descending colon near the splenic flexure. A suture is placed to repair a small serosal tear in the splenic flexure area, ensuring adequate length to reach the top of the rectal stump.
